title: Konjunktiv I (Reported Speech)
summary: Konjunktiv I is used in formal reported speech to convey what someone said without implying it is true. It is common in journalism, academic writing, and official discourse.

sections.heading: Forming Konjunktiv I
body: Konjunktiv I is formed from the infinitive stem. For most verbs, add the endings -e, -est, -e, -en, -et, -en to the stem.
The verb 'sein' is highly irregular and must be memorised: ich sei, du sei(e)st, er/sie/es sei, wir seien, ihr seiet, sie seien.
For 'haben': ich habe, du habest, er habe, wir haben, ihr habet, sie haben.
Note: the ich-form of most regular verbs (e.g. ich gehe, ich komme) is identical to the indicative, which causes ambiguity — see the section on when to use Konjunktiv II instead.

examples.de: Er gehe jeden Tag ins Büro.
examples.en: He goes to the office every day. (reported)

sections.heading: Using Konjunktiv I in Reported Speech
body: In formal contexts, reported speech is introduced by a verb of saying or reporting (sagen, berichten, erklären, behaupten) followed by a subordinate clause with the verb in Konjunktiv I.
The conjunction 'dass' may be included (with verb-final order) or omitted (with V2 order and Konjunktiv I marking the indirect speech).
Past events in reported speech use the perfect-tense Konjunktiv I: auxiliary (habe/sei) in Konjunktiv I + past participle.

examples.de: Sie berichtet, das Experiment habe funktioniert.
examples.en: She reports that the experiment worked.

examples.de: Der Minister erklärte, die Lage sei unter Kontrolle.
examples.en: The minister declared that the situation was under control.

examples.de: Die Sprecherin teilte mit, dass alle Beteiligten informiert worden seien.
examples.en: The spokesperson announced that all parties involved had been informed.

sections.heading: When to Use Konjunktiv II Instead
body: If a Konjunktiv I form is identical to the indicative, the reported-speech signal is lost. In such cases, Konjunktiv II (or the 'würde + infinitive' construction) is used instead to preserve the indirect-speech meaning.
This problem arises particularly in the 1st person singular and in the 1st/3rd person plural, where Konjunktiv I forms overlap with indicative forms for most verbs.
Example: 'ich gehe' and 'wir gehen' are the same in both indicative and Konjunktiv I for 'gehen', so reporters write 'ich ginge' or 'ich würde gehen' instead.
In everyday speech, 'dass'-clauses with indicative are common; Konjunktiv I is primarily a written/formal register feature.

examples.de: Sie sagten, sie kämen morgen. (not: *sie kommen)
examples.en: They said they would come tomorrow. (Konjunktiv II used because 'kommen' Konj. I = indicative for wir/sie)

examples.de: Ich erklärte, ich würde pünktlich erscheinen.
examples.en: I explained that I would appear on time.

examples.de: Die Forscher behaupteten, die Ergebnisse seien eindeutig. (sei/seien is unambiguously Konj. I)
examples.en: The researchers claimed the results were clear.

exercises.prompt: Der Zeuge sagte, er ___ die Person nicht kennen. (kennen → Konjunktiv I, 3rd sg.)
exercises.explanation: Konjunktiv I of 'kennen' in the 3rd person singular is 'kenne' (stem 'kenn-' + ending '-e').

exercises.question: Which form correctly completes the reported speech? Der Sprecher erklärte, die Verhandlungen ___ erfolgreich gewesen.
exercises.explanation: 'seien' is Konjunktiv I of 'sein' (3rd person plural), appropriate for formal reported speech. 'Wären' is Konjunktiv II, used only when Konjunktiv I is ambiguous.

exercises.prompt: Die Ministerin berichtete, das neue Gesetz ___ nächsten Monat in Kraft. (treten → Konjunktiv I, 3rd sg.)
exercises.explanation: Konjunktiv I of 'treten' in the 3rd person singular is 'trete'.

exercises.question: Why would a journalist write 'sie kämen' instead of 'sie kommen' in reported speech?
options: Because 'kommen' has no Konjunktiv I form
Because the Konjunktiv I form 'kommen' is identical to the indicative, so Konjunktiv II is used to avoid ambiguity
Because Konjunktiv I is only used in the singular
Because 'kämen' is actually the indicative past tense
exercises.explanation: When Konjunktiv I is identical to the indicative (as with 'wir/sie kommen'), Konjunktiv II ('kämen') is substituted to clearly signal reported speech.
In reported speech without 'dass', the verb stays in second position within the reported clause; Konjunktiv I 'habe' signals indirect speech.
